text.title: Business Analysis Planning Failure Modes
text.summary: 4 antipatterns observed in real applications of this methodology. Each pairs symptom + root cause + concrete fix.

antipattern.symptom: T2 lists plausible-sounding roles ('Director of UX', 'VP of Platform') that do not exist in the org chart.
antipattern.root-cause: LLMs hallucinate stakeholder roles based on training distributions; the gap to reality causes missed sign-offs at critical decision points.
antipattern.fix: Cross-check every T2 entry against the org-chart CSV before approving; reject any name not on the list.

antipattern.symptom: Single 'BA Plan' document is produced; T4 information-management and T5 performance-metrics are silently dropped.
antipattern.root-cause: T4 and T5 are the least visible BABOK tasks; LLMs drop them because they appear in fewer training examples.
antipattern.fix: Validate against the five-artefact schema; reject when any of T1-T5 is missing.

antipattern.symptom: T3 governance lists approvers as 'leadership' or 'the product team'.
antipattern.root-cause: Vague approvers cannot sign off; escalation paths point to a role, not a person.
antipattern.fix: Require an email or @-handle per approver; reject role-only outputs.

antipattern.symptom: T5 metrics ship with invented baselines like 'rework_rate=12%' on the first cycle when no data has been collected.
antipattern.root-cause: Invented baselines are post-hoc rationalisations; the first cycle is for measurement establishment.
antipattern.fix: Force baseline=null and target=null on the first cycle; only populate after two cycles of actual data.
